era_context: Middle Bronze Age. Babylonia under Samsu-iluna. Egypt's 13th Dynasty is in decline. The Hittite Old Kingdom is consolidating. The Indus Valley Civilization is at its mature phase. This is a period of regional stability with localized conflicts and trade networks functioning across Eurasia.

events.title: Samsu-iluna's reign (early-middle period)
events.description: Samsu-iluna, son of Hammurabi, continues his reign as king of Babylon. Cuneiform records document administrative and military activities during this period. The Old Babylonian period is characterized by cuneiform administrative records, legal codes, and military campaigns. Specific events for this year are not preserved in surviving sources.
events.certainty_note: Samsu-iluna's reign is documented, but specific events for individual years are rarely recorded. Chronological uncertainty remains ±50 years depending on which chronological system is used.

events.title: Hittite Old Kingdom consolidation in Anatolia
events.description: The Hittite Old Kingdom continues its consolidation of power in central Anatolia. Archaeological evidence from Hattusa shows ongoing palace construction and administrative development. The Hittites are establishing themselves as a major regional power during this period.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Hattusa (palace remains, administrative tablets)

events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology for the Old Kingdom is uncertain; this assignment is conventional and may be off by ±30 years.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization at mature phase
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues at its mature phase, with large urban centers such as Mohenjo-daro and Harappa functioning as administrative and trade hubs. Archaeological evidence shows sophisticated urban planning, standardized weights and measures, and extensive trade networks extending to Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf. The civilization's undeciphered writing system is used on seals and pottery.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The mature Harappan phase is well-established archaeologically, dated c. 2500–1900 BCE. This assignment is within that range.

events.title: Egyptian 13th Dynasty fragmentation
events.description: The 13th Dynasty of Egypt continues to experience loss of centralized control. Regional governors (nomarchs) in Upper and Lower Egypt are consolidating local power. This period marks the transition toward the Second Intermediate Period, when Egypt will be divided among multiple competing dynasties. Archaeological and textual evidence shows administrative fragmentation.

sources.name: Administrative papyri and inscriptions from Upper Egypt

events.certainty_note: The 13th Dynasty is poorly documented; the Turin Papyrus lists many kings but their chronological order and reign lengths are disputed. This assignment reflects the general period of fragmentation.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the general narrative of 'consolidation' in the Hittite Old Kingdom and 'fragmentation' in Egypt may be oversimplified. The exact timing and nature of these processes are debated among scholars. The Egyptian 13th Dynasty is so poorly documented that some scholars question whether it represents a continuous dynasty or a collection of competing regional rulers.
historiographic_note: This year is characterized by very sparse documentation. No specific events are recorded for -1824 in surviving sources. The entries above reflect general trends during this period rather than documented events. Mesopotamian cuneiform records are the most abundant source material, but they are undated in absolute terms. Egyptian sources are fragmentary. The Indus Valley Civilization left no deciphered written records. The absence of contemporary records from most regions means that major developments in China, the Americas, and sub-Saharan Africa are entirely invisible. Older scholarship heavily favored Mesopotamian and Egyptian sources; more recent work has attempted to integrate Hittite, Indus Valley, and other regional evidence, but significant gaps remain.

graph_edges.note: Samsu-iluna's reign continues; his policies shape Babylonian development.
The Hittite Old Kingdom consolidation eventually leads to the New Kingdom expansion.
The decline of the 13th Dynasty eventually leads to the Second Intermediate Period and the rise of the 15th Dynasty (Hyksos).
